data.title: JD.com Has An Involution Problem
data.description:
JD.Com (NASDAQ:JD) is an attractive stock. 9 out of the last 10 Seeking Alpha articles have rated it a "buy" or "strong buy" and only two contributors have rated it a "sell" in the last three years. It's hard to argue with the consensus, but its performance does not equate with the strong fundamentals.

At the current price of $32.69, it is trading at 2019 levels and even at prices traded in 2014, its first year on the Nasdaq.

This article looks at why such a good company has such a bad performance.

JD Overview

JD is, at its core, an online marketplace operating mostly in China. Its limited international reach sets it apart from its main competitors, Alibaba (BABA) and Pinduoduo (PDD), as does its ownership of inventory and logistics.

Q1 earnings were released in May and showed the company is in good shape. Revenues are growing in double digits.

Free cash flow was nearly $3B in Q1, and EPS is growing YoY, although a dip is expected this year.

Margins are fairly tight, which is a concern, but also something that can be improved on going forward.

Non-GAAP operating margin was 3.9% for the first quarter of 2025, compared to 3.4% for the first quarter of 2024. Operating margin of JD Retail before unallocated items was 4.9% for the first quarter of 2025, compared to 4.1% for the first quarter of 2024.

Infrastructure has expanded, and the company is gaining a foothold in the EU through Poland. Domestic activity has also expanded, and in March, "JDL officially launched its operations center in Hong Kong, marking a significant step-up in expanding the coverage of its express delivery network and boosting service efficiency in the region."

Increased efficiency and coverage could help improve margins.

The two things that really stand out are JD's strong finances and its low valuation. Its forward PE ratio is only 10.44, compared to BABA at 12.77 and PDD at 12.4. Amazon (AMZN) has a PE ratio of 35.9.

You have to wonder why it is so low. This isn't a case of being under the radar - just look at the coverage on Seeking Alpha. It relates to the extremely challenging deflationary environment.

The China Problem

The US trade war with China is a weight on the Chinese economy, which was already fragile. While tariffs have been reduced from the extremes of early April, they are 35% higher this year and could weigh on activity.

The tariffs will affect international exporters such as PDD more than JD, but a sluggish economy will not help domestic consumer spending, which is already weak and leading to deflation. CPI turned negative in mid-2023 and has slipped back into negative territory again in 2025.

ING included this commentary with the above chart:

One of the main reasons is a contractionary cycle featuring heavy price competition as well as pay freezes and pay cuts. This will be a difficult challenge to tackle. Policymakers, though, recently shifted attention toward addressing the problem, with an eye on improving market exit mechanisms, encouraging consolidation and restructuring, and addressing non-market practices resulting in excessive price competition.

"Excessive price competition" is bad news for JD, which is in a low-margin business with prices racing to the bottom. Even worse, it owns its inventory, which is losing value over time.

Involution

"Involution" is a term that has been around for years and generally describes intense competition within a group that leads to stagnation and burnout. It's generally a societal experience, but is also being used to describe business practices in China, where prices are aggressively discounted to the detriment of the companies involved. Policymakers in Beijing have started using the term more and are looking to address this unhealthy price competition.

At the Davos World Economic Forum in June, China's Premier Li Qiang pledged to make the country a "mega-sized consumption powerhouse." Details were scant, but the government and central bank have a variety of tools at their disposal to boost consumer demand. Rate cuts are an option, but due to the deflationary environment, yields have been on a path lower anyway, and a cut in the short end won't be a quick fix.

Li also said China's development was "not a short-term expansion but a steady progression toward long-term goals." It does seem they are playing the long game, and the (mostly) self-inflicted popping of the property bubble shows they can pain in the short-term.

Assuming the government does now address involution and tackle deflation, JD would be well placed for further growth and could demand a higher multiple. This is a solid company operating under a very challenging retail environment that may be about to change.

Outlook

The last major JD rally came in Q4 2024 when the government last announced stimulus. This move broke the downtrend, but soon ran out of steam and drifted lower again.

Earnings have been solid, but aren't having a sustained effect on price moves. JD seems to be hanging around for the next big announcement and we don't know what or when that will be. The risk is that price drifts lower, but it may be worth taking a position in advance of any news. $26-27 is where the price broke out of the downtrend last year and is a good starting point. Upside targets are initially at the $48 area and the "double top," but I do think JD can break this resistance and move higher into the $50s.

Risks

Beijing is playing the long game and seems unconcerned with short-term pain. Addressing involution could initially have a negative effect. Higher prices could suppress demand further.

JD has good finances so it should be able to withstand a further slump, but its stock price may return to the 2024 low of $19 where it has found support many times over the years. If that breaks, it would signal some deep problems.

Conclusions

JD is a profitable company with strong finances and an attractive valuation. However, the stock is drifting lower as the environment for retail in China is extremely challenging. Excessive price competition driven by soft demand is a major issue, but this "involution" is something policymakers in Beijing have recently vowed to address. Unfortunately, the government are playing the long game and there is no quick fix. I therefore continue to rate JD a "hold" as it could lack direction for some time. $26 seems an attractive buy-in point in anticipation of a better macro backdrop in 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gladstone Investment: A Fair Price For A Great Business
data.description:
Valuation has been a recurring theme of my coverage over the past several months. The valuation of assets across the market has been stretched to levels that I call priced to perfection. Looking at the S&P 500, the earnings yield is now considerably below the risk free rate. According to Multpl, the S&P is now trading at 29.9x earnings, or an earnings yield of 3.3%. Compared to a ten year treasury that yields nearly 4.5%, this looks like an extraordinary price to pay for the largest companies in the United States.

Looking elsewhere, we'll see that assets look similarly expensive. Credit spreads have compressed considerably since the depths of the tariff madness when bond prices began to decline. Today, spreads have tightened considerably, even in the high yield space. This means those who are searching for value are forced to begin looking elsewhere. The traditional big two asset classes look stretched. Equities are unlikely to surge again with their valuations baking in major earnings growth over the next several years. Bonds need rates to fall considerably to deliver the "equity-like" returns that investors seek from high performing fixed income. Two tantalizing catalysts that are keeping investors on their feet as the market continues to froth into 2025.

As it feels fair to stick to the theme of valuation, we are going to explore a sector off the beaten path. Looking beyond the large capitalization names carrying the market and bonds that offer modest income, we are going to seek returns in a highly valued market. Accordingly, we must get creative and begin looking where others are not. Enter stage - business development companies. Today's discussion will center around Gladstone Investment (NASDAQ:GAIN), a long-tenured business development company that maintains a reasonable valuation in a rich market. We will explore the company's price to book multiple and the recent acquisition of a major security infrastructure company.

Setting The Stage

Gasp! Even business development companies are looking expensive these days. Following the short lived panic caused by the tariff madness, the market has hit its stride and risk has gone out the window. As I just mentioned, equities and fixed income are both looking relatively expensive and we are venturing into the weeds to find some value. This means exploring other sectors that might be less familiar to investors, like business development companies or REITs. As is always the case, not all players are created equal.

In fact, I would argue that some of the top names in the industry are looking similarly overvalued in a frothy marketplace defined by sky high expectations for growth. Let's look at two specific examples.

First, let's examine one of the largest participants in the sector, Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC). In February, I published an article on ARCC discussing the company's Q4 earnings release and corresponding pricing.

To put it succinctly, ARCC has little room for upside, trading at a compressed yield and expanded price to book value. While there's no indication that the company is running into trouble, I believe there is little upside to be found by investing at current prices.

Today, ARCC continues to trade at 1.16x forward book value, around 10% higher than their historical average of 1.05x book value. Typically with ARCC, patiently waiting for an opportunity to purchase at or below book value has been favorable.

More recently, I just published an article on Main Street Capital (MAIN), one of the most well known brands in the sector.

Unfortunately, MAIN is priced exuberantly at around double its book value. Historically, this has set the company up for a sharp price decline, as occurred in March and April.

If shares of MAIN are to return to their historical mean valuation of around 1.6x book value, there is once again a 20% decline in our future. At the current price to book value to 2x, the multiple is around 25%-30% higher than historical averages. I would also argue that this comes during a time carrying more risk for BDCs, not less.

What if an investor wants an equity heavy BDC priced at a reasonable valuation? They should look no further than GAIN.

Review Of Company & Coverage

GAIN is a publicly traded business development company from the Gladstone fund family. The company manages other BDCs and REITs in niche sectors. Perhaps most notable is the company's farmland REIT, Gladstone Land (LAND). GAIN focuses on the acquisition, recapitalization, and ongoing management of mature companies in the middle market across several segments, consumer products, and business/consumer services. GAIN does not invest in startups, preferring to target mature, stable companies.

GAIN typically makes debt investments between $5 million and $30 million and equity investments between $10 million and $40 million. GAIN actively targets companies with revenue in the range of $20 million and $100 million and EBITDA from $4 million to $15 million. GAIN seeks minority ownership of targets and prefers to hold a board seat. GAIN's typical hold period is seven years with an exit via recapitalization, initial public offering, or sale to third party.

I have covered GAIN on a variety of occasions in the past. Most recently, I published an article on GAIN in 2024, opening up the company's business model and explaining why the company was positioned to perform well in a defrosting IPO landscape.

GAIN is one of few truly unique operators in the BDC space. While business development companies are typically a dime a dozen, GAIN is a private equity investor hiding as a public company. For shareholders, this means an opportunity to capitalize on private investments in a liquid, scalable, and transparent format.

GAIN continues to trade at a small premium to net asset value, currently 1.06x. However, this premium/discount is more complex than meets the eye. Given the large allocation to equity investments, GAIN's portfolio is likely worth more than current NAV would suggest. GAIN continues to be a 'buy' off of strong tailwinds and an impressive strategy that continues to deliver.

Since publication, GAIN has returned 24% to shareholders on a combination of share appreciation and distributions paid. After an active year of acquisitions and dispositions, GAIN has delivered to shareholders in more way than one. Below are GAIN's one year price and total return.

GAIN's emphasis on mature businesses has created stability and long term growth for GAIN. Let's explore this piece of the business further before diving into the valuation.

The Gladstone Difference

GAIN takes a largely differentiated approach to BDC investing. While most business development companies function mainly as lenders for companies, GAIN takes a different style. While the company still loans to portfolio buisnesses, their less-than-secret weapon is the company's equity investments in portfolio businesses. In their acquisitions and recapitlizations, GAIN also makes an equity investment and genreally seeks a board position.

GAIN specifies four primary characteristics they like to see in prospective acquisiton targets: Experienced Management, Value-Orientation/Positive Cash Flow, Strong Competitive Positioning, and Enterprise Collateral Value. This four spoke startegy helps identify companies that are mature and "boring" in terms of predictable growth and cash flow.

The company typically targets an equity investment totaling 25% of their portfolio. Below is an excerpt from the cmopany's most recent annual report.

We expect that our investment portfolio over time will consist of approximately 75% in debt investments and 25% in equity investments, at cost. As of March 31, 2025, our investment portfolio was comprised of 73.3% in debt investments and 26.7% in equity investments, at cost.

Combined with participating preferred and the company's equity positon rises to more than one third of the portfolio.

So, what does a typical deal look like for GAIN?

Let's explore the company's recent investment in Global Grab Technologies. Global Grab is a provider of security systems for infrastructure around the world. This includes perimeter security and vehicle mitigation (think surveillance, fences, and guard gates around airports, etc). GAIN's investment included a combined $67.6 million debt and equity package to the company.

While Global Grab's balance sheet is not readily available to investors, infrastructure security is a stable industry in a world where dynamic threats are growing and emerging.

These acquisitions are part and parcel to GAIN's business model. The acquisition and eventual disposition of a company like Global Grab lays the groundwork for the company's significant special distributions which have occurred for several years. Most recently, GAIN paid a special dividend of $0.54 per share in June.

[object HTMLElement]

The Valuation Is Appealing

So, having reviewed the company's business model, the question becomes what makes GAIN attractive relative to other BDCs. Well, let's explore…

From my chair, there are a limited number of BDCs that are like GAIN in terms of strategy or execution. The company's equity emphasis means many traditional BDCs are not necessarily all that comparable given they have some slightly different performance drivers. It is generally important to be looking apples to apples when making comparisons.

I see Main Street Capital (MAIN) as one of the most comparable business development companies to GAIN. Setting aside the differences in management structure (internal vs external) and balance sheet growth (GAIN is considerably smaller), there are significant overlaps.

Both companies target the middle market with an equity kicker to their investments. Both companies target old, established businesses with stable operations. Both have generated impressive returns on equity over long time periods. Looking over long time horizons, GAIN has been the superior pick to MAIN. GAIN has outperformed MAIN handily over the past decade, establishing a winning 10-year period.

More recently, MAIN has overtaken GAIN as a golden child in business development companies. However, we should look at the source of this outperformance. Let's cut the riff raff and get right to it. Over the past year, MAIN has outshined GAIN considerable due to an expanding valuation.

As of today, MAIN trades over 2x book value based on the company's recently released estimates. Despite non-accruals continuing to rise to some of the highest levels in the industry, MAIN shares are powering ahead with exuberance. This situation led me to publish an article yesterday explaining why MAIN could see another double digit decline in the near future.

Meanwhile, GAIN is trading at book value, priced just 5% above the company's estimated net asset value. While MAIN's performance has come largely on account of investor enthusiasm tending higher, GAIN has actually delivered to shareholders in distributable cash flow and NAV growth. Better yet, the company is actually trading below its historical mean valuation of 1.06x book value.

This leaves these two BDCs in a radically different position. GAIN is still firing on all cylinders and has plenty of room to run as management continues to remain active in redeploying the proceeds of portfolio exits. Trading in line with book value provides a fair price to enter this great business.

Outlook

Another important piece of the GAIN puzzle is the outlook around private equity. The company's equity heavy strategy means business valuation is as important as the ability of portfolio companies to repay their indebtedness to GAIN. Luckily for us, private equity is heating up after several years of weakness that have challenged the market. Let's explore deeper.

Ernst & Young recently published their private equity pulse, which provides a market overview of buyouts and mergers. In Q1, deal volume increased by 45% year over year, driving overall deal value to more than twice the comparable period from the prior year.

Even more specifically, EY sees opportunity emerging in the middle market, GAIN's backyard. The company says that opportunities are likely to emerge across the U.S. middle market with manufacturing businesses taking the lead.

Conclusion

The BDC segment is large and getting larger. As companies continue to emerge and join the growing industry, sniffing out quality amidst growing competition becomes increasingly important. As with REITs, not all businesses are created equal. Generally, I recommend shying away from the new kids on the block, showing favor to some of the industry's established competitors instead. This has worked out well with companies like GAIN, MAIN, and ARCC giving healthy returns to shareholders over long time frames. As the industry continues to perform well, GAIN earns a Buy.

data.title: DuPont: Neutral Rating Was Correct - Looking Post-Qnity
data.description:
I invest quite a bit in chemical companies, and world-leading ones are sort of at the top of my list in the sector. These include companies like BASF SE (OTCQX:BASFY), which are generally the leaders in larger parts of the business, but also specific dominating leaders like Brenntag (OTCPK:BNTGY), which focuses on distribution. However, it also involves leaders like DuPont de Nemours, Inc. (NYSE:DD), a company steeped in a strong history and tradition. I have shares in the business, but following challenges in the sector went neutral on the company and gave it a hold about 1.5 years ago. Since that time, I have not bought more, nor have I sold off the entirety of my position (I sold 50% in the fall last year at a decent profit, but unfortunately not all).

The company has returned 4.6% in over 1.5 years, which is not great, given that the S&P 500 (SP500, SPY) is up 32%+ in the meantime, giving us the following performance.

As you can see, the right choice would have been to take profits in October last year, which I should have done with more conviction. Now, the company has recovered from a short-term decline, and I mean to update my thesis for the future.

DD is not the highest-yielding chemical business, but I will make the argument that it is one of the safest, and one that could have an upside at this time.

Why?

Let's take a look.

Dupont - Update for 1Q25 performance and the upside in 2025-2027E

We have the latest set of results, which are now roughly 1.5 months old. Some changes from my 2024 thesis are warranted, given the changes that DD seems to be intent on making to its portfolio. It's no wonder the company is doing this, given a 200+ year history and a portfolio of chemicals that has evolved with time and demand.

The company's current plan is to spin off its electronics business as the next sort of step in its "growth/change phase." This business will be called Qnity, which will leave DuPont with some key technologies. This includes safety and protection like Kevlar (doubt they are ever giving that up), as well as Tyvek. Both of these technologies are legacy, and both are used in key segments such as textile and construction, generating over $8B in sales, with around 40% of a pro-forma amount of annual sales coming from legacy protection (protection includes Tyvek also).

The new pro forma DuPont will see a heavier correlation to housing, which is currently set to grow with nearly 1.5M starts in the next 10 years. The remaining 60% of the new revenue will come from specific end markets, including healthcare, water, and mobility, at about 20% each. You also know I love chemical companies that work with water - this is actually one of the reasons I pushed into DuPont in the first place.

On paper, this company will therefore serve a diverse set of end markets, with an (in my opinion) very appealing overall mix. The end markets include, but are not limited to, healthcare, EV, aerospace, construction, and machinery, with water especially being something I focus on, given the increased need for water filtration as well as water "generation" (from things like salt water and the like). I think we'll see more companies pushing into this.

Overall, I'm not negative on this change - I view it as potentially appealing, even though I have a bit harder time following the decision rationale. Strategic focus and unlocking value are mentioned, with increased agility for the company, as well as shareholder value.

I'm personally not convinced, but time will tell - I don't view it as something that will impact what remains in DuPont negatively, however.

That being said, I'm not that interested in investing in Qnity - the reason I invested in DuPont was not the electronics sector, generating a pro-forma standalone $4.3B, but the other businesses. Qnity, to give a quick wrap-up, will focus on chemicals specific to semis, and interconnect solutions - meaning Wifi/5G. Qnity will be correlated to semi demand and semi-industry trends. I will keep an eye on it, but I have businesses that I consider to be more interesting in this industry.

Arguments for this change and for DuPont concerns, unlocking value and effective capital deployment alongside attractive electronics positioning. I prefer to focus on the appeal of DuPont's legacy, where Tyvek and Kevlar are two technologies I view as reasons #1 and #2 for investing in DuPont while they unlock further value in other areas. Both of these legacy chemicals/technologies have attractive profit margins and have the sort of market presence and pricing power that I doubt will decline over the years. I consider it sort of an American Sika AG (OTCPK:SXYAY), a Swiss company in building sealants that I cover.

The risks, which I will cover in more detail, however, are that DuPont's spin-off does not actually result in any financial efficiencies or gain, instead resulting in operational challenges, market volatility - and we also know that any spin-off is always a process. Even the company itself acknowledges these risks.

To add further to this, DuPont still has billions in potential PFAS-related liabilities, which now have a smaller operational base to generate cash flow for coverage, so consider that as well, as it does impact value and contribute to some of the volatility we're seeing in the company.

Fundamentally, DuPont is attractive and, based on its legacy operations, certainly has a moat, albeit a small one (because it's not exactly overinvesting in new tech through high R&D). And while comparing DuPont to more basic material-chemicals businesses like BASF can only be done carefully and the company is undervalued next to its closest peers, we also need to consider the company's risks.

Highlights in Q1 include good growth and solid margin increase. It's what caused the company's relatively swift bounce up. EPS increased 30% on an adjusted basis, and the company made the plans for Qnity more concrete.

Tariffs certainly carry an impact potential for the company, but DuPont is moving to reduce it.

However, the above also illustrates the difficulty even for a huge company to do just that.

2025E guidance calls for net sales of just south of $13B for the complete company, with an operating EBITDA of up to $3.375B, and adjusted EPS of at least $4.3/share. That would mean a 5-6% EPS growth for the year.

DuPont is BBB+ rated, is a very low-leverage company, and remains a company that more often than not beats estimates, at least over the past 10 years. It's a company that I view as often being underestimated - that's one of the reasons I bought it when I did, but I also try not to overstate what's possible for the business, and this is one of the reasons I sold 50% of my position about 9 months ago. The company's fundamentals are attractive, but the yield at 2.2% is sub-par to most of the chemical companies I invest in. This results in a relatively "narrow" corridor of appeal.

My estimate forward includes Qnity as part of the company, and I have not yet decided what to do about the Qnity shares - currently, I tend towards the decision to sell them, if I still hold DD shares during the spinoff phase.

Evaluating forward potential is tricky. DuPont has underperformed the market since I last wrote about it, and most indicators tell me that the company is unlikely to outperform.

Indicators such as the ones below, which include the valuation.

Valuation for DuPont - Upside to a fair value of $90/share, but I'm more hesitant about the P/E and about growth

In my last article, I put the company's fair value at about $80/share, which, given the upside available back then, was insufficient, leading to my Hold rating. The same is unfortunately true at this time.

The company trades at below $75/share, but the trouble comes when we start estimating forward growth. On a 20-year basis, the company manages about 16-17x P/E, making it 16.5x. The company's current average weighted earnings multiple is 17.73x, which comes to a slight overvaluation. Positive arguments for the company include the EPS growth potential - about 10-11% per year in 2026E and 2027E - but to that I say that we don't know at this time how much will be Qnity (which is supposedly spun off this year) and how much legacy DuPont. Furthermore, risk factors also include PFAS litigation as well as tariffs, neither of which I believe are fully realized and included in the current estimates for double-digit growth.

The one argument we have is the company's tendency to outperform estimates, but over time, this has not helped DD investors to outperform the market. In fact, as you can see, for every single specific period, DD has underperformed the SPY, which you can also see on the Seeking Alpha tool here.

Therefore, I say that investment valuation, meaning the value at which you buy, is absolutely crucial. I would not buy DD above its 15-20 year multiple, and with the added uncertainty, I would not compromise on this one inch. I'm maintaining $80/share fair value at this time, but I put the price target ("PT") at $68/share or about 16.5x current earnings, when I see a conservative upside of 15% annualized or above, inclusive of the yield. That's the earnings-related thesis. Your current potential RoR looks as follows, based on a 20-year average.

Peers can offer us some insight and alternatives. The company's direct peers are businesses like 3M (MMM) and Xylem (XYL), both of which are currently overvalued as well. Value can instead be found in basic materials, chemicals, and/or distribution. My choice at this time, if you were to be interested, is German Brenntag SE (OTCPK:BNTGF). The company trades at sub-14%, has a 1.5% higher yield than DD, is also BBB+ rated, and works only with distribution. Operational results are set to grow by over 12% per year over the next 3 years, and at the same multiple as DD, the company has an annualized upside of over 23%.

That makes this company, in my view, a better choice here, which Is also why I invest in the business.

The risks for my thesis are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The primary risk for my neutral rating is one of outperformance from the Qnity spinoff. Investors could see significant returns if the stocks of both companies appreciated higher than the individual values of these segments as they are today, which is also part of what I believe the company counts on. Beyond this, I view DD's end markets and operations as especially characteristic of not delivering surprises, as we can see from the company's long-term results and comparisons to the SPY.

A higher PT would therefore, to me, entail a higher likelihood for post-Qnity outperformance.

My thesis for the company is as follows.

Thesis

  • Chemicals/Industrials are still an investable segment, and I continue to invest major capital in businesses such as BASF and Brenntag, among others.
  • DD continues to combine the appeal of specialty chemicals with a great market position, excellent fundamentals, and a yield that could still increase, even if the current plan makes it more likely that growth is to come from different ways (specifically, a spin-off). My PT for the company reflects the company in its current, not-spunoff state.
  • I give DuPont a "HOLD" with a PT of $68/share from a fair value of $80/share for the longer term, legitimizing some of the company's premium that we see here, but not as much as in my previous article from 2024. I would be careful going higher, and view other companies, such as Brenntag, as materially more attractive.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $68/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of July 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
